source_metadata.title_raw: Albert S. Yang - A-18-074 - June 19, 2018 - Palo Alto

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• I 1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Albert S. Yang 
Deputy City Attorney 
Office of the City Attorney 
250 Hamilton Avenue, 8th Floor 
Palo Alto, CA 94301 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-074 
Dear Mr. Yang: 
June 19, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Palo Alto Assistant 
Director of Planning and Community Environment Jonathan Lait (the "Assistant Director") 
regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the ''Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re 
Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and 
accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should 
contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit the Assistant Director from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to the Castilleja School's pending development applications given that his spouse's solo 
psychotherapy practice (the '"Psychotherapy Practice") receives referrals of potential clients from 
the School as a result of being on its psychotherapy referral list? 
CONCLUSION 
No. The Act does not prohibit the Assistant Director from taking part in those decisions 
because they would not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on his financial interests in 
the Psychotherapy Practice or his financial interest in his personal finances. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 I 000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR 
File No. A-18-074 
Page No. 2 
You are a Deputy City Attorney for the City of Palo Alto and the authorized representative 
of the Assistant Director. The Assistant Director's job duties include supervising the processing of 
applications for public and private development within the City. 
The Castilleja School is a private, all-girl middle and high school located within the City. In 
June of 2016, the School began submitting development applications to the City with the objective 
ofrenovating the School's existing facilities to increase its enrollment. The School's current 
enrollment is approximately 438 students, and the proposed renovation would enable an increase of 
approximately I 00 additional students. 
At the end of 2017, approximately six months ago, the Assistant Director's spouse opened 
the Psychotherapy Practice, a solo psychotherapy practice, with an initial focus on counseling for 
teenagers and young adults. In early 2018, the Assistant Director's spouse had several local schools, 
including the Castilleja School, list the Practice on their respective psychotherapy referral lists. 
Although the Castilleja School does not maintain a physical psychotherapy referral list, it 
regularly makes referrals to approximately eight different psychotherapy providers that it has 
interviewed and prescreened, including the Psychotherapy Practice. There is no queue of listed 
providers. If a student or his or her guardian seeks a psychotherapy referral, the Castilleja School 
Counselor assesses the student's needs and usually recommends three of those eight providers 
based on the assessment and the availability of the providers. The School never recommends only 
one provider. The student or guardian who sought the referral retains complete discretion over 
whether to further pursue treatment and the selection of a psychotherapy provider. There is no 
consideration exchanged between the School and the providers on its psychotherapy referral list to 
join or remain on the list. 
The Psychotherapy Practice has received and will likely continue to receive client referrals 
from the Castilleja School, and referred clients may ultimately comprise a significant portion of the 
Practice's client base. The Practice does not receive any income directly from the School,"and its 
fees are paid entirely by its clients. You state that the decisions relating to the Catilleja School's 
development applications are entirely unrelated to the School's inclusion of the Psychotherapy 
Practice on its psychotherapy referral list. You have asked whether the Act's conflict of interest 
provisions disqualify the Assistant Director from taking part in those decisions given that the 
Practice receives referrals of potential clients from the School. 
ANALYSIS 
. The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a 
governmental decision that would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or 
more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
(Section 87100 and 87103.) An official's interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of 
interest under the Act are identified in Section 87103. Of those interests, the facts presented indicate 
that the Assistant Director has the following interests with respect to decisions relating to the 
Castilleja School's pending development applications: 


File No. A-18-074 
Page No. 3 
• 
A business interest in the Psychotherapy Practice. Section 87103(b) provides that an official has 
an interest in any business in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or 
more. For purposes of Section 87103, an "indirect interest" means, among other things, any 
interest owned by a public official's spouse. Therefore, the Assistant Director has a business 
interest in the Practice, assuming his spouse's interest in the business is worth $2,000 or more. 
• 
A source of income interest in the Psvchotherapy Practice. An official has an interest in any 
source of income aggregating $500 or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to 
the official in the 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) The Act's definition of 
"income" includes any community-property interest in the income of a spouse and a pro rata 
share of the income of any business in which the individual or spouse owns a 10-percent or 
greater interest. (Section 82030(a).) Therefore, assuming the Assistant Director will receive 
$500 or more in community-property income from the Practice in the 12 months prior to the 
decisions at issue, he has a source of income interest in that business.2 
• 
An interest in his personal finances. An official always has an interest in his or her personal 
finances or those of immediate family members under Section 87103. 
Thus, with respect to the decisions relating to the Castilleja School's pending development 
applications, the Assistant Director has a business interest in his spouse's Psychotherapy Practice, 
including a source of income interest in that business, and an interest in his personal finances. 3 
Foreseeability 
Regulation 18701 sets forth the standards for determining whether a decision's effect on an 
official's interest is reasonably foreseeable. If the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in the 
decision, the decision's effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701 (a).) an 
official's interest is "explicitly involved" in a decision if the interest is a "named party in, or the 
subject of," that decision, and an official's interest is the "subject of' a decision "if the decision 
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other 
entitlement to, or contract with, the [official's] interest." (Ibid.) The Assistant Director's interests in 
the Psychotherapy Practice are not explicitly involved in the decisions relating to the Castilleja 
School's pending development applications because the business is not a named party in or the 
subject of those decisions. 
With respect to an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the 'decision at issue, 
Regulation 1870l(b) provides that the decision's effect on the interest is reasonably foreseeable ifit 
"can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." If the City 
2 The Assistant Director would also have a source of income interest in any client of the Psychotherapy 
Practice from whom he receives $500 or more in community-property income through the Practice. Because the facts 
presented provide no indication that the Assistant Director has a source of income interest in any client of the Practice 
or that the decisions at issue implicate a client, we do not analyze those decisions' effect on any client of the Practice as 
a source of income to the Assistant Director. 
3 However, where a decision affects an interest in a business entity or real property, an effect on the official's 
interest in his or her personal finances need not be considered under Regulation 18702.S(c). Accordingly, we do not 
further address the potential effect on the Assistant Director's interest in his personal finances. 


File No. A-18-074 
Page No. 4 
approves the Castilleja School's development applications, the School will be authorized to 
renovate its facilities. The renovated facilities would enable the Castilleja School to increase its 
enrollment. While increasing enrollment by l 00 students may lead to additional referrals to 
providers listed on the School's psychotherapy referral list, it is unlikely that the small number of 
additional referrals will lead to a significant increase in the Psychotherapy Practice's business. 
Based on your facts, the potential effect of the decisions at issue on the Assistant Director's 
interests in the Practice does not appear to be "more than hypothetical or theoretical." 
We note that, pursuant to 1870l(b)(l), the following factor should be considered in 
determining whether a decision will have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a financial 
interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision: "The extent to which the occurrence of the 
financial effect is contingent upon intervening events, not including future governmental decisions 
by the official's agency, or any other agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of.the 
official's agency." 
Your facts reveal that many intervening events must occur for the Psychotherapy Practice to 
realize a financial effect from any increase in Castilleja's student population. For example, upon a 
student or his or her guardian's request for a psychotherapy referral, the Castilleja School Counselor 
makes recommendations based on his or her assessment of the student's needs and the availability 
of the listed providers, including the Psychotherapy Practice. The Counselor always makes multiple 
recommendations to a student or guardian seeking a referral, and the ultimate decision to become a 
provider's client rests with the student or guardian. Because the Counselor always makes multiple 
recommendations and selects the providers to recommend from the list, and because the student or 
guardian who seeks a referral retains complete discretion over whether to further pursue treatment 
and the selection of a provider, if any, we believe this chain of events breaks the link of 
foreseeability that the effect of the decision has on the Assistant Director's financial interest. 
Therefore, the decisions relating to the Castilleja School's pending development 
applications would not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect upon the Assistant Director's 
financial interests in the Psychotherapy Practice because those decisions' effect is not more than 
hypothetical or theoretical at this time. Thus, the Act does not prohibit the Assistant Director from 
talcing part in those decisions. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Acting General Counsel 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division
